speaker: interviewer
text: October 23, 1996, survivor Pola Grinstein, G-R-I-N-S-T-E-I-N. Interviewer Rita Reines Jacobsohn, J-A-C-O-B-S-O-H-N. We are in Miami Beach, USA, and language-- interview is going to be conducted in English. And this is tape number 1.

speaker: interviewer
text: October 23, 1996, survivor Pola Grinstein Shteinshneid. I'm Rita Reines Jacobsohn. We are in Miami Beach, USA. The interview is going to be conducted in English, and this is tape number 1.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Mrs. Grinstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: We shall begin by talking about the town where you came from. But first, tell me where you were-- when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Ostrozec in 1925, December-- 15 of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name, can you spell it for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name, Pola, P-O-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shteinshneid, S-H-T-E-I-N-S-H-N-E-I-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. You said you were born in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrozec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is near Lutsk-- 16 kilometers from Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about Ostrozec?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a shtetl, about 1,000 families-- maybe less, maybe more. And we had three temples. And we had a school-- a public school. And we had private teachers, which they will teach Hebrew. And-- and privately, all kinds of religious things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: None. In the shtetl, there was none not Jewish. Around was-- the village, [INAUDIBLE] Ostrozec, and there was mostly Ukrainian, and some Polish teachers, or from the post office, was living there. But mostly Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Jewish people and non-Jewish people do business-- they had business together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they used to come-- they didn't have business together. They used to come to buy something in the shtetl. Everything they used to buy, to come to the Jewish people, Jewish stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- for instance, it was this super-- not supermarket-- grocery stores. And was stores-- materials for clothing. And so nothing more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father-- what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: David.

speaker: interviewee
text: David Shteinshneid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was this forest-- used to buy forest, and to make out all kind of material from the forest, from the tress.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example?

speaker: interviewee
text: For instance, for the-- for the railroad-- how you say--

speaker: interviewer
text: The tracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the-- the wood. And for furniture, for eating-- everything had he sell [INAUDIBLE]. And for building, also. And then we had a farm, And he used to buy-- we had a Count Liderhowsky-- a Polish count. The count was Polish, a very good man. He used-- every winter, my father used to go, and he used to donate some wood for the poor people. Potatoes he used to give, and some flour for the poor people-- twice a year, and for Easter.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say poor people, who were they? People that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very poor people. For instance, there was these widows, which their husbands died. There was no social security. Was no help for nobody. My mother used to collect bread, sugar, or some other things, and used to put down by the door that she shouldn't even know where was given. She was ashamed to take, you know? And we used-- when was a wedding or a bar mitzvah, or a child was born, I helped my mother to make such a-- to pins, and you give a donation, [NON-ENGLISH]. And for these donations, they went for [INAUDIBLE], and then some to help out people, poor people. This was their [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my stepmother. But this was my-- my mother's name was Milka, my stepmother's. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Milka what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milka Shteinshneid-- from home, Milka Katz, I think, Katz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was your stepmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was a year old when she came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaya [? Goula. ?] Chaya Goula.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chaya Goula.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Chaya Goula. But they used to call her Goula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Milamed ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- she died when my birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the fifth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the fifth child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father remarried?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a year, he remarried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your siblings, what are the names of your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest was Rivka. My brother was Chaim. My sister was Minde-- Mindel. And my fourth-- fourth sibling was Sarah. She died when she was 7 years old. And me. And then was born by my stepmother-- she couldn't have children. She was the-- her husband divorced her after 10 years because she couldn't have. And that time, they didn't know the reason. Always was the fault of the woman. He divorced her. My father didn't want more children. Then he married her. She was a very nice-looking woman. And to the year, she had a child. And this was my brother. We grew up together, I with him. We were very, very close. Only with them, I called her mother, and they called her [NON-ENGLISH]-- aunt. And I didn't know that she's not my mother until 8 years I was old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eli. Eli.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: My middle sister, Mindel, she was very jealous of me. I was very rotten spoiled. And I used to beat her sometime up, you know, and my older sister, there were big differences.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much older were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the oldest was maybe 13 years older. I don't know. No, 12 years. Until I know that she was already engaged. And my brother also was much older. This sister also. And then the other sister, and my mother was-- heart trouble, she had. The doctor told her she shouldn't have no children anymore. She'll die. She was very religious, and she told him, I don't want no abortion to make. And I'll have what God will give. Then I was alive and she died. Then she-- I made too much trouble, and she say, you killed my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was 8 years old. And I think, why? No, every little thing, I say, I have a mother. I'll tell my mother. I'll tell my mother. And you know, my mother used to stick with me, because you know, she didn't take part between us. We used to beat-- the brother and me, I used to beat him up. I was older, two years older. But she didn't want to [? mix in. ?] And then we made up and we kissed. Then she say, you killed my mother. I said, why, why? Because when you-- she had you, and you killed her. Then I wept. I start to cry, and everything. All year, I was very, very unhappy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since then, I start, you know, to-- this was already not a mother, then a stepmother. [INAUDIBLE]. Before she was very good. I was very happy. And since then, I said oh, with my mother, and I missed my mother. I didn't have no picture, nothing. They hide it from me, everything. I shouldn't know. I don't know my mother, even from a picture. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Up to now. And you started calling her--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, mother, I called her. Then after one year, you know I saw, and I understand. And I was, you know, but still-- not this, what was. At night, I didn't sleep. I want to see my mother, at least in sleep. And then-- but we-- when Hitler came, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler came?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ran away together, I and my mother and the brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the prewar years, when you were growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember school, going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember very good, going to school. It was a big anti-Semitism. They're all-- the Ukrainian and the Polish, saying [NON-ENGLISH]-- Jews to Palestine. Go back to Palestine. They used to beat us up. And it was very bad. But we didn't have no choice. We needed to go. Some teachers, you know, protect us, and some, you know, like they didn't hear or didn't see, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of subjects did you learn in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the subjects, you know. Polish was the main subject. Polish, geography-- not nature--

speaker: interviewer
text: Science?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, mathematics were very, very important. And then gymnastics very, also. And no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Needlepoint?

speaker: interviewee
text: Needlepoint. And also this drawing. No, we had a lot. This is true. We had a lot more to do. Every day before we went to gymnastic, we made-- and once a day, many, many gymnastic put out the nerves, the energy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite subject was this [NON-ENGLISH], and this history. History was my favorite. And the languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides Polish, did you learn other languages in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- Ukrainian, I learned at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at home, what language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hebrew, Yiddish, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hebrew and Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spoke with your family, with your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. Only Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about a nice holiday that you remember from home.

speaker: interviewee
text: A nice holiday-- my father, you know, because he was, you know-- in that time, he was the richest man in our shtetl. He had the farm, and we had this forest business, and the lumber business. And our shul was down. Then he built it up, renovated it. And he was like [INAUDIBLE] gabbi in shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a special name, the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Olyka-- Olyka Shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Olyka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Olyka because Olyka had a rebbe. They called him Olyka Rebbe like Lubavitcher rebbe, because he was from Lvov. Olyka Rebbe-- my father was Olyka Rebbe Hasid. And the shul was Olyka Rebbe's name. And he used to come to the shtetl to visit the Hasidim. He used to stay by us. He gave me my [INAUDIBLE], and a brucha, a blessing. I was very sick. He gave me a blessing. I was usually a very sick child-- wild, spoiled, and sick-- a very sick child. And he told, don't worry. She'll grow. She'll be well, she'll be-- God will help her. She'll overcome. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then he used to go back to Olyka. In Olyka, I had my sister married to Olyka. I had already a married sister with two children. The sister's name was Rivka. My brother-in-law's was Chema. The children was [? Manya, ?] Miriam, and the boy was Chaim. Also they called him [? Munia. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Family name-- Dreishbun. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far is Olyka from Ostrozec?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same like from Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like 15, 15, 16 kilometers. The same. We used to go very often. Then my brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- with horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- how you say-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Carriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: A carriage and horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went often there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they came every vacation, every summer, they came out on the farm. And they came to us, like [INAUDIBLE]. And then I had a brother married to [INAUDIBLE] Olyka. And he had a wife, Hannah Glickman from [INAUDIBLE], and two children, a son, Berela, and a daughter, Ruchela. And they used to come. I used to go there very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home, the remaining children-- who were the remaining children at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home was Mindel, me, and my brother, because the other died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the one that died when she was  7 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was before Passover. We ate together. We ate supper, and she told us that she has stomachaches. And we had a Dr. Romanov. She told us-- she was a friend with my father, with my mother, and she told us secretly that she is from the family from the Czar Romanov.

speaker: interviewer
text: The doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: The doctor. And she wanted to teach me Russian. I didn't want. She spoke Russian. I spoke Polish. And she say, one day, maybe you need to use the Russian language. And then when the Russians came, she said, you see? I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was right. Could you learn any Russian at all? Did you learn any Russian at all, with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know Russian, not at all. I didn't like the Russian language. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far were you able to go to school before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: How far? Very near. It was a small shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- which grade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven before the Russians came.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember when the Russians came-- the Russians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- one moment. I think also June. No-- one moment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think June. It was in June? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: June '40-- no. This was '39. This was in '39. In '39, we [INAUDIBLE] Poland and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '39. I don't remember the month.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home, did you hear your father and your mother talking about it, about what was going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother didn't like politics. But my father was a big politician. He and a pharmacist, [? Solis, ?] they used to talk, and the radio to listen. And he was very good on the map and in geography.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was so-- and I picked up from them, you know? And from-- you know, from [INAUDIBLE] and Madame [? Peresterova, ?] how they were spies, and they sold out Poland because they were Germans. And she didn't-- wasn't interested in politics. But you heard your father talking about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And if my grandfather came, he was also a politician. He was a very religious man. My grandfather write the Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A sofer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sofer Torah. He was a businessman. This is his hobby. Four Sefer Torahs he wrote in his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grandfather's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moishe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe Shteinshneide.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was from your father's side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And from mother's side, they told me that he was also the same, a sofer. But this was his-- he make a living from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when grandpa came-- how did you call your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zayde.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zayde.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zayde Baba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Baba?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baba Zayde.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Baba's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baba Cyril.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cyril?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cyril Shteinshneide.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they came, and you heard him talking about what was going on in Europe at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. My brother was a big politician also. In fact, my brother was-- he finished rabbi in the [INAUDIBLE] Yeshiva. But he didn't want to be a rabbi. He was the leader from the [INAUDIBLE]. He was very religious, very religious. He was big, big fan Hasid from Olyka Rebbe. And he was in Olyka every Saturday. He used to go to eat with the Rebbe [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they dress in a special way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The Yeshiva in [INAUDIBLE], he was a rabbi that his father finished the [INAUDIBLE] Yeshiva. He didn't shave, but he-- with a special machine, you're allowed. And my father, like Lenin, a beard here, [INAUDIBLE] small. No payos. No, my brother also no payos. But a yarmulke in the head, he had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not, [INAUDIBLE]. No. Nice-- this suit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were Hasidim from the Olyka's rebbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before that there was a lot of anti-Semitism when you were going to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember one time where you had to go through it, or witness anti-Semitic--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time, of course. Especially in school. You know, they were so-- I didn't, because we had workers. They couldn't be anti-Semitic, because my father would have thrown them out, you know. They make a living from my father. And the workers in the forest also. But when I went to school, they didn't know. They know, or they didn't know. To each other, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I can-- I had a big satisfaction. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The big satisfaction when I came to Israel, and I [? saw ?] the Polacks, how they work, and now they're happy to be in Palestine, Israel, and to make money by the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to the prewar years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, what kind of literature could you find in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH] and the [NON-ENGLISH], two Jewish papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father read them to you? You read them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I read them. No, I liked-- that time, I liked books. I read a little bit, but my father used to tell me. I used to ask him. He liked to discuss with me. He liked to discuss. I was-- he told-- he always told that I-- I have his head, because my brother hate business. He didn't like business terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the Yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH] didn't like business. He married a business lady. And she was in the business, and he helped her out. And my sister married, and my fourth, Mindel, she-- my mother didn't know even what kind of business my father has. And I-- I can explain from the farm, and I can explain from the forest, what from which tree you can make out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how well informed you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used to go with my father. He used to take me when I had the vacation. I used to take girlfriends with me too. And they used to explain. No, I ask it. Not only explain, I ask it. [? Daddy, ?] why you make from this? You say this furniture. He'd say furniture needs to be-- should be this, you know. And for eating, there must be the [INAUDIBLE] for eating the [INAUDIBLE] wood, you know, for fall out, yes. And he explained-- explained me, and I know it. And I know he showed me how to measure, and how to make the material-- everything. And I liked this. And when I come I on the farm, then I look at everything. You know, I took, for instance-- they make, you know, like for all kind-- and come to-- [NON-ENGLISH]. How you say [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: To cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, when they take off the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The collection of the-- recollection of the produce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: The recollection of the produce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wheat-- wheat-- the produce, yes. Then I remember everything by the fruit trees, how they make, cultivate the time-- everything. Because they [INAUDIBLE] trees and they cultivate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What changes occurred in your town when Hitler gained power?

speaker: interviewee
text: When Hitler gained power, right away--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going-- this is-- we're going to stop with this question and going to change tapes now. And you tell me on the next one.
